LEADERS & TEACHERS OF TODAY—11
‘BE SILENT, MR TADPOLE’ *
Winston Churchill and
Greatness in Politics
by C. B. Purdom
This is the eleventh of C. B. Purdom’s scrutinies of the work
and ideas of current leadership. In his twelfth—and final
—article C. B. Purdom will, next month, endeavour to
crystallize into pattern and meaning the kaleidoscopic
philosophical outlook of our times.

THERE IS NO MAN, with one exception, who has
created more controversy in English politics over the
past thirty years than Winston Churchill, and there is
no man, without exception, who brings more listeners
to the radio when he broadcasts to-day. Lloyd
George has been a larger controversial figure and has
enjoyed higher office than his younger contemporary,
but he has hardly aroused deeper feelings and to-day
he arouses none at all.
The two men are a whole class apart, though they
both possess dynamic energy, audacity, and elasticity
of mind. It is not my intention to compare or contrast
them, however; it is sufficient to remember that
Winston Churchill is a soldier, while Lloyd George
is a lawyer.
That Mr Churchill was brought up and trained as a
soldier is the first thing to note about him. Thus he
gained power of command, a quality that he possesses
in outstanding measure. He can decide and act, as
he has proved time and again. If he had wisdom too,
he would be an ideal leader; but in the quality of wis-
dom he has deficiencies: his energies are unrestrained,
his tongue unruly, and he is indifferent to reputation.
He belongs to the great tradition of English politics,
and in the world of Fox and Burke he would be entirely
at home. When we listen to his voice we can be sure
that we hear tones equal to those which came from the
great Parliamentarians of the past. No one else speaks
to-day in those accents. When I hear him rolling out
his eloquent lisping periods, I say to myself that we
are listening to the swan-song of Parliament.
Yes, I will go so far as to say that; for while he
moves and excites and amuses us, in the midst of
admiration for his gusto and mastery, we are sharply
aware that his is a lone voice, that the polite, dry,
unimpassioned lectures we are accustomed to get from
the other politicians, which neither touch our hearts
nor give us confidence, present a truer picture of the
age.
Yet let us be glad that he was born on 30th November
1874, descendant of the great Duke of Marlborough,
whose biographer he became. He entered the Army
at the age of twenty-one, served with the Spanish Army
in Cuba against America, served also in India, Egypt,
and South Africa. During the Boer War he changed
his profession and became a war correspondent, was
* Retort by Churchill to carping criticism in the House of
Commons.
2
taken prisoner, but escaped. Then he changed his
profession again, and became a politician; having failed
in 1899 to win Oldham, he was successful there a year
later as a Conservative. Tariff Reform caused him to
change his politics and in 1906 he was returned for a
Manchester constituency as a Liberal. His long
ministerial career began at thirty-two, when he became
Under Secretary for the Colonies; at thirty-four,
President of the Board of Trade; at thirty-six, Home
Secretary; at thirty-eight, First Lord of the Admiralty,
which he was when the war with Germany broke out.
He left the Government in 1915, changing back into
a soldier, holding a command in Belgium; but he
changed once more in 1917, when he returned as
Minister of Munitions, followed in 1919 by the Ministry
of War, and in 1921 by the Secretaryship for the
Colonies. Another metamorphosis occurred in 1924
when he was returned as a Conservative for Epping
and became Chancellor of the Exchequer in the second
Baldwin Government. He held this office till 1929,
when he went into the wilderness and remained out of
office for ten years, the re-starting of the war in 1939
bringing him back to the Admiralty, much to public
satisfaction.
These changes do not indicate uncertainty or lack
of principle in the man, but prove his tireless energy.

[Abbildung: Porträt von Winston Churchill]

He holds his own wherever he is, and is not to be over-
come.
He has performed many spectacular parts. The
Battle of Sidney Street, when he was Home Secretary,
will never be forgotten; his expedition to Antwerp in
1914; his Russian adventures in 1919; his restoration
of the gold standard in 1925; his part in the General
Strike in 1926, and the figure he nearly cut in the
abdication of Edward VIII will also be remembered
—some to his credit, some not.
He has jeopardized his career many times, for if hot
water is there, he is in it. His description of Ramsay
MacDonald as the ‘Boneless Wonder’ during the
second Labour Government helped afterwards to keep
him out of office, as Mr Malcolm Muggeridge reminds
us in his entertaining book, The Thirties, and he is ever
likely to do or say the unexpected thing. He is not
a ‘Yes-man,’ and that reason is regarded as un-
reliable by the Powers that Be.
He is deeply versed in constitutional matters and
on such questions as the Statute of Westminster, the
Government of India, and the Abdication, what he has
said is deserving of study.

He is a voluminous writer and an admirable journalist.
Accustomed to preparing his speeches with care he
puts the same skill into his writing, and the same elo-
quence too. He is not to be regarded as a writer of
model English; for his style is flamboyant and aimed too
cunningly at effect. He undertakes great themes of
national polity but the style will decompose the matter.
Burke was a great speaker, but not an eloquent one;
his style was better suited to writing than to speech.
The contrary is true of Churchill. He emulates
Burke, but lacks Burke’s literary discipline, almost the
only discipline he had. Churchill has no such disci-
pline, so that to read him once is to read him no more.
However, a man cannot have all the virtues, and
certainly to listen to Churchill is better than, by all
accounts, it was to listen to Burke.
What is it that gives his wireless talks so strong an
appeal? There are people who hate them: most people
regard them as the best tonic of the War. He speaks
with assurance, frankly, and with studied unreserve.
There is an air of indiscretion combined with authority.
There is no touch of self-apology. He employs
oratorical skill subdued to the medium of the micro-
phone. But while these qualities help to make the talks
successful, what puts them in the first class is something
else: throughout, there is more than is expressed in
actual words, a tone of conviction, of inflexible purpose,
of certainty, as though the cause were already won.
This is not boastfulness, though he does boast, nor is
it arrogance, though he is arrogant; it is something
the voices of hardly any of our other politicians have,
and certainly none who speak for other nations, Hitler
least of all. It is something characteristically English,
a quality instantly recognized, the sign of the peculiar
quality of our national spirit. The same tone is to be
heard throughout England distinguished in common
speech, and echoed in the daily talk of men and women.
That is why the response to Churchill is so eager; he
utters what the ordinary man thinks in accents that the
ordinary man recognizes to be true.
There are those who prophesy that he will end at
10 Downing Street. Of all the figures on the political
horizon, his is most like what a Prime Minister’s
should be. In politics the unexpected happens, and
in these times, with the world upside down, it is in the
unexpected that we shall find succour.

A FRESH BREEZE
IN AN OLD HOUSE
‘British Painting since Whistler’

IF OUR STANDARD of cultural values was anything
but topsy-turvy, every paper in Fleet Street would be
blazing away with front-page headlines about what is
happening at the home of Old Masters. We should
all be talking of it.
The National Gallery has ‘gone contemporary.’ In
October the place was thrown open for a series of lunch-
time concerts: a timely effort to fulfil a clearly-expressed
need. Now, surprisingly, it stages an exhibition which
is an equally courageous attempt to answer a question
about contemporary painting. ‘What,’ it asks the lay-
man, ‘is the relationship between you and painting?’
‘British Painting since Whistler’ is the modest title of
this startlingly good show of paintings produced by
British artists during the last forty years or so; but it
might have been called ‘A Hope for British Painting’
—if only enough support is given by people of taste and
judgment to make it clear that more and bigger shows
of the same kind are wanted all over the country.
The exhibition’s chief value is that it proves that
British art, far from being dead—or useless—is alive,
healthy, and kicking strongly. What is more, normally
sensitive and intelligent people will be able to feel the
kicks just as easily as experts and connoisseurs. And
herein lies the hope for our culture: if the arts and
everyday life are closely linked, civilization may survive.
Otherwise it is inevitably doomed. This show can do
a tremendous amount to prove that art still matters to
the man-in-the-street. There is nothing stale, musty,
arty or lah-de-dah about these paintings. Remarkably
few of them are the work of pedantic octogenarian

[Abbildung: The Slum von James Pryde]

The Slum James Pryde
R.A.’s. But another good thing, equally remarkable,
is the absence of incomprehensible abstractions such as
have given the layman the notion that art has nothing
to do with him. And it is good to note the marked
individuality of nearly every painter: no slavish imita-
tion of French or other models here.
There is no truth in the popular idea that artists are
3

FRESH BREEZE IN AN OLD HOUSE (continued)
simply cranks practising an obscure and demoded craft
for private and personal satisfaction—for sheer amuse-
ment either of themselves or of a small clique of ‘en-
lightened’ admirers. Artists, as this exhibition makes
abundantly evident, are people intensely interested in
the visual world around them, who are anxious to
communicate to others, similarly interested—to all who
like using their eyes—something of the emotion they
themselves experience. Rarely, however, we are able
to see a comprehensive collection of contemporary
work, to discover in what sources our painters now find
inspiration.
But here at the National Gallery you may see for
sixpence the best work of many of the best artists of
our time, painted for their own amusement—because
they like using their eyes, too—and for ours. They are
not a race apart: only they happen to possess vision
and powers of observation, more acute, more carefully
trained than ours, and the controlled power to express
their peculiar experience of things seen, imagined, or
dreamed by painting or drawing. It is a pleasure no
thoughtful, sensitive person should deny himself—to
go to this exhibition with both eyes open and his mind
on the move, remembering simply that their object in
painting these pictures is precisely that of all artists of
all time—to express emotion and to communicate
experience to ordinary people. They all have something
definite to say, ideas worth expressing and worth

[Abbildung: Seafaring von Edward Wadsworth]

Seafaring Edward Wadsworth
4
absorbing. No one spending an hour here can fail
to add to his understanding and enjoyment of the out-
side world. As with books, so with pictures. Some
authors appeal to one reader and leave another cold.
So here some pictures will have an obvious message for
you and a less obvious message for me. Some of them
may leave us both wondering. . . . But some at least
of these painters will put across new ideas you will wish
to retain and ponder. There is variety to suit every
one’s taste.
This is not a ‘revolutionary’ exhibition chock-full of
repressed ‘isms,’ but it is good to note that besides the
established institutions whose standards have always
included at least technical excellence, most of the more
progressive groups of painters receive plenty of atten-
tion. The painters within these groups have contri-
buted a tremendous amount of work that will stimulate
the imagination of any one who appreciates a Capra, a
Lubitsch, or a Hitchcock film or (dare one say?) a Bix
Beiderbecke record.
Some of the best work of Wilson Steer, Henry Tonks,
Walter Sickert, Augustus John, and other members of
the New English Art Club is here. Charles Ginner,
Bernard Meninsky, Mark Gertler, R. O. Dunlop, and
other London group exhibitors are well represented.
So are John and Paul Nash, and Edward Wadsworth,
who helped to form Unit One. But the pictures are
hung, not in groups or cliques, but individually—and
all must stand on their own merit. On every wall is
something eloquent, significant, alive: a couple of
bunches of bright yellow daffodils full of sun—their
vivid colour heightened by the rich ultramarine back-
ground; a sombre, sordid slum tenement with a dignity
no palace ever had by reason of a patch of sunlight
slanting across it; Richmond Castle in shimmering
light; Don Quixote on Rozinante, solitary beneath a
murky sky; the Thames at Battersea in misty silvery
sunset; cascades of magnolias sprawling in prodigal
profusion across a landscape; two bits-o’-skirt posing
before the stop-me-and-buy-one cameraman at Hamp-
stead; mallard rising from the water with an almost
audible wing-beat; no camera can approach the ima-
ginative treatment of these and countless other paintings
here.
The variety of styles is just as exciting as the range of
subjects painted. Stanley Spencer takes such great
delight in a field of green corn (The Bridle Path, 174)
that he meticulously paints every blade, every leaf, every
petal in the foreground and carries his enthusiastic
precision throughout the entire picture, communicating
effortlessly the perfection of little things mingled in a
wide sweep of earth and sky. Vanessa Bell is thrilled
by the brightness of a garden in spring sunshine (The
Walled Garden, 114), and expresses her pleasure with a
broad, freely-handled brush. Mark Gertler paints a
noble, sympathetic portrait of his mother (136) in rich,
warm tones and well-rounded forms. Augustus John’s
swift, restless brush sums up the imperious, self-con-
fident character of Lady Ottoline Morrell (192) in a
satirical study that almost hisses derision. But you ’re
going to see this show, I hope, so that laboured
description is unnecessary.
This ought to be merely a beginning; it should lead
to many similar exhibitions in the provinces—might

even bring good contemporary painting into every town
in the country. To look still farther ahead, it might
conceivably be the forerunner to a Renaissance of
painting so far-reaching as to enable the man-in-the-
street to buy for his own home paintings of value and
interest instead of letting the furniture man supply ‘a
few pictures thrown in’! LPTB use Nevinson,
Epstein, Kauffer; maybe you and I will be able to
commission sketches and studies from less well-known
artists some day. If the demand is great enough the
supply will grow—and the prices drop—accordingly.
The National Gallery has set the ball rolling. Is it
too much to consider it a fair test of our democracy’s
satisfactory cultural condition whether the ball rolls on
or stops? Anyway, it ’s up to us now. The collection
is there, in the middle of London. If we are really as
interested as we ought to be, there won’t be the slightest
doubt about the support it will receive: the crowds will
be as big and as eager after two months as during the
first week. And in consequence the exhibition may
continue considerably longer than the tentative two
months originally planned. Then we must all of us
demand many more of the same kind, all over the place
—if we believe that art has everything to do with life
because it is an integral part of that very culture and
civilization for which we are all fighting. . . . Milton
was not indulging in over-statement when he said:
‘Where no arts flourish, where all learning is exter-
minated, there is no trace of a good man, but cruelty
and horrid barbarism stalk abroad.’ S. G. C.

[Abbildung: The Pond von Mark Gertler]

The Pond Mark Gertler
The illustrations here, including the portrait on the front
cover, are reproduced from ‘British Painting since Whistler,’
by permission of the National Gallery.

TWO WAYS OF KNOWING
Is Radio versus Reading?

THE BOOK WORLD has for years been anxious
about the competition of radio with reading. It
would seem a matter of simple arithmetic to conclude
that if a family spends most of its time with its ears
strained towards the radio its consumption of printed
matter must fall.
The first authoritative survey of radio and reading
has lately occupied the moneys of the Rockefeller
Foundation and the energies of Princeton University.
Two years and thousands of interviews have been
spent in probing the public’s relative addiction to
reading and to listening; and the effect of one upon
the other.
The size of the radio audience is almost beyond the
conception of the book world; it staggers our not-too-
robust imaginations. In the United States nine out
of ten families own radios and forty-five million sets
bellow, chatter, or make chromatic noise for an average
of five hours a day. ‘Radio,’ says Reader’s Digest, ‘has
reached in twenty years the goal towards which print
has been working for five hundred.’
Not only do their people listen but they write letters.
U.S. Congressmen were mighty impressed when, as
the result of radio debating on the Neutrality Bill, they
received a fan-mail of one hundred thousand letters.
Here, they exclaimed, was the voice of the people,
and you know what the voice of the people is. Until
someone pointed out that a commercial broadcaster
(putting over some soothing-syrup) received a fan-mail
of two and a half million letters, it looked as if Demo-
cracy had words for it. The Congressmen pondered
also on the fact that a half-million response to a radio
advertising was almost a commonplace.
The survey’s conclusion on this point, as the Con-
gressmen had to concede, was that a large part of the
radio audience was composed of suckers, to use a
native term, and was infinitely responsive to suggestion.
This response comes from the lower-income groups,
who, because they cut off from the excitement that
pounds, shillings, and pence give them, and because
radio is cheaper than books, spend far more time listen-
ing than the higher-income groups.
In the better income groups the Princeton survey
discovered that radio listening was not only more
selective but that the reading type of person listened
more to the type of programme which competed with
reading—talks, debates, commentary, discussion, and
then went and read more.
Whoever’s to blame for it—and the man who
invented the terms highbrow and lowbrow should be
first in the dock—the conclusion that, intellectually,
the rich get richer and the poor get children seems
indicated. Nevertheless, it would be a wrong con-
clusion and democracy can yet take heart.
If the investigators found that radio made readers
read more, they appear to have found no evidence that
it made non-readers read less. In short, constant
5

TWO WAYS OF KNOWING (continued)
listening to radio—especially if it is radio of the British
quality in which a sense of responsibility persists—will
at some point arouse intellectual curiosity. A steady
inclination towards intellectual progress is inevitable,
a slow turnover towards mental liveliness.
For radio (and films too, as anyone who has seen
‘the film of the book’ will appreciate) leaves a sense of
dissatisfaction. While the programme lasts, the excite-
ment is intense, but visual or oral communication
possesses quick evaporative qualities. Radio and
films, as yet, have not the fine subtleties of evocation
which thousands of years of writing have evolved.
Radio must, because of its large audiences, conform
to the idea that it must be inoffensive; radio presentation
must be short, patchy; and it cannot (as Duhamel has
pointed out) adjust its speed of dissemination, as does
reading, to the individual. Nor is there with radio
any return to a knotty problem. The Reader’s Digest
sums it up in a sentence: ‘Reading is a more efficient
process than listening.’
So books remain unique and (despite the obituaries
of Tom Hanlin) reading has not yet taken the count.
More, it can expect help from the radio and the films.
If that help ropes in the thousands, and does not im-
mediately turn the millions into dons, then let us be
content with the thousands: for once converted they
will not return, and each man or woman—as General
Temperley told those who would feed our army’s
intellect on fripperies—has a leadership value in
disproportion to his numerical insignificance.
J. B.

READING THE CLASSICS AGAIN—14
ANTHONY TROLLOPE’S
AUTOBIOGRAPHY

ANTHONY TROLLOPE was born in London on
the 24th April, 1815, and died there 6th December
1882. The Autobiography was published a year later,
in 1883.
He was the third son of Frances and Thomas
Trollope. His mother was one of the first to capitalize
the art of debunking—her Domestic Manners of the
Americans gained her a reputation which she followed
up with a series of novels which gained great public
favour, but are forgotten to-day. Thomas Trollope
was a Chancery barrister of excellent probity; but
unhappily possessed of an ungovernable temper which
set everyone at odds with him. Every venture,
from an Encyclopaedia Ecclesiastica to farming—for
which he had no aptitude whatever—failed miserably.
The family suffered dire poverty for years, and Trollope
records his childhood and youth as being utterly
miserable.
Awkward, unhappy, ill-clothed, inapt at sports,
Anthony Trollope soon became the butt of schoolboy
sneers and ill-mannered jokes. He could not afford
tips to the school servants, and got black looks; and
the master of his form never forgot to point Trollope
out as the only non-payer. Dr Butler, Headmaster of
Harrow, once stopped Trollope in the street, bluntly
telling him he was the dirtiest boy in the school.
Eventually the family had to flee the country to
escape imprisonment for debt; and there Frances
Trollope wrote pot-boiling novels in the intervals
snatched from tending the death-beds of her husband
and second son. From this misery Anthony was res-
cued—if the fire can be said to be better than the
frying-pan—by being given a post office clerkship.
It was only £90 a year, and he was expected to live
on it. But he could not; got into debt, was unable to
pay his lodging expenses; and, being lonely and friend-
less, fell into bad company. About this, however,
he says nothing beyond the bare mention of it, and
6
deploring it. His office work suffered in consequence;
despite constant threats of dismissal, he found it
impossible to work. Besides, owing to his office
chief’s dislike of him, Trollope was never given an
opportunity to prove his worth. But he had a sturdy
independence of spirit, and when once charged with
theft, thumped the chief’s table with such emphasis,
that his chief was deluged with ink.
He entered the Post Office in 1834; seven years later
(1841) he went to Ireland as a post office surveyor.
This gained him a salary, which, with expenses,
amounted to £400 a year. In Ireland he found himself;
he learnt his work, became passionately fond of it, in
fact; and, from being despised, was soon accorded
high praise for his efficiency. He also became an
ardent huntsman, though an indifferent horseman,
and very short-sighted.
In the lean years of friendlessness and poverty at
home, abroad, and in the Post Office, Trollope had
one consolation. He built up a dream-world of happi-
ness, replete with friends who loved him, office chiefs
who praised his work, of being esteemed on every
hand. But he kept it in strict control; there were no
antres vast or desarts idle therein; he carried it on for
years, but always within the limits of possibility.
He was thus, all unconsciously, a novelist in day-
dreams; and when, in the happy years of his official
and married life in Ireland, he at last satisfied his urge
to write novels, he was merely putting on paper what
he had so often dreamed of in unhappier days.
But he had no exaggerated ideas of his merits. He
was prepared for failure, and so did not despair. He
first achieved publication in 1847, with The MacDermots
of Ballycloran, but not until The Warden (1855) was
published did he attain any public encouragement.
When he came to sum up his earnings as a writer in his
Autobiography, they amounted to the very handsome
sum—in Victorian sovereigns—of nearly £69,000.
Trollope is another example of the great public
servant who was also a man of letters—one can draw a
parallel between his life and Chaucer’s in this respect.
His postal work carried him all over the country, which
gave him abundant opportunity to observe human
frailties and foibles; in the hunting field, the office,
in town and country life, he was always observant;

and his imagination worked the raw material into novels
which are as alive and enchanting as when they were
written almost a hundred years ago.
He was chosen to go abroad to Egypt, the West
Indies, to North and South America, as ambassador
and negotiator for the Post Office; and was eminently
successful. As a public servant he always obeyed
orders—but unhesitatingly and scathingly criticized
official fatuousness. He incurred the enmity of Sir
Rowland Hill by his advocacy of the liberty of civil
servants to hold independent views. He gave a lecture
to some post office clerks on this subject, and Hill
urged Trollope’s dismissal. But he was too valuable
a servant to be curtly or summarily dismissed, and
Hill had to swallow his chagrin.
When the Autobiography was published Trollope’s
admirers were sadly taken aback by the matter-of-fact
way in which he described his method of writing novels.
Somehow it seemed a degradation of the creative
spirit to work by the hour like any day-labourer. But
anyone who has attempted writing will come to see
how wise, just, and excellent was his method; in no
other way can a writer get his books written. With
usage, a writer can command his creative imagination,
so that when he sits at his table he hasn’t to nibble his
pen. Tyro and hardened scribbler alike can profit by
his advice—it is born of experience and wisdom.
His novels have been described as ‘wise, tender, and
unpretentious’; the same words are applicable to
Trollope. Closing the Autobiography leaves one with
the impression of a most winning, gracious, and sin-
cerely humble man, one who underrated his work.
He did not expect posterity to remember him, and says
so without hedging the question. But we who read
him know better, appreciating his true worth.
Of his novels, Nathaniel Hawthorne said:
Have you read the novels of Anthony Trollope? They
precisely suit my taste—solid and substantial, written on
the strength of beef and through the inspiration of ale, and
just as real as if some giant had hewn a great lump out of
the earth and put it in a glass case, with all its inhabitants
going about their daily business, and not suspecting that
they were being made a show of. . . . It needs an English
residence to make them thoroughly comprehensible; but
still I think that human nature would give them success
anywhere.
Posterity has given them success; Trollope is the
social historian of the Victorian Age; his views on
the ethics and economics of marriage, for instance, are
as penetrating and plainly expressed as Shaw’s.
You can admire Thackeray, Dickens, the Brontës,
and George Eliot, but you feel you can go further with
Trollope—you can love him; he is so amiable and
human. There is a warmth of feeling in his literary
style which is almost unique in English letters:
Though deep yet clear, though gentle yet not dull,
Strong without rage, without o’erflowing full.
Above all, Trollope has a genuine sense of humour;
he makes you laugh, chuckle, roar, giggle—run the
whole gamut of humorous reaction.
D. M.
The Autobiography of Anthony Trollope. World’s Classics,
Oxford University Press, No. 239, 2s. 6d.

Members are permitted to purchase an extra copy of RU choices
or additionals but application through suppliers should be made
early to ensure supply: a month before publication in the case of
choices and with the usual application card in the case of addi-
tionals. Many members have purchased extra copies of Still. Back
numbers of Readers’ News to complete files or replace losses can
often be supplied free on receipt of postage. We are also always
glad to post sample copies of Readers’ News to friends of members.
Some sets of the compact and pleasant reprint of Biology for
Everyman—produced in response to a clamorous demand—still,
to our surprise, remain on our stock shelves. If there’s a pur-
chase to be made of a pre-war standard in every respect, it’s for
that book. At 7s. the set of nearly 1,600 pages, and every opening
packed with honest learning and good illustration, J. Arthur

I teach a Special Class
Had the report of Charles Russell’s reactions kept to the
defects of his pupils I should not have felt obliged to write this.
I would have mused: ‘Another Sleeping Beauty has been kissed!’
But he did more! He has the impudence to criticize elementary
schools and magistrates, when, from the evidence he supplied
in his article, he does not even know his own job properly.
Let us examine his findings. They run roughly like this:
(a) Sensational discovery of a ‘moronic’ class; (b) its existence
due to the elementary schools, the school attendance officers, and
the magistrates.
As the second is quickly disposed of, let us deal with it first.
‘This moronic class owes its existence to the elementary school,
the school attendance authorities, and the magistrates.’ This is
the equivalent of saying that the rubbish owes its existence to the
midden! An examination of the elementary school will
confirm this.
Practically all poorer and middle-class children attend the
elementary school, and the education they obtain is superior to
that obtained anywhere else I know. I have taught both elemen-
tary school children and the product of the private schools. But,
at the age of eleven, all the children in an elementary school are
examined. About ten per cent win scholarships to the secondary
school, and forty per cent go to the central or selective senior
schools and technical schools. The remaining fifty per cent
are left to continue their education at the elementary school.
Obviously, the milk has been skimmed with a vengeance!
When we get to the class leaving school, the cleverest children
get the jobs, and the remainder, the dregs of the school, go
on the dole and attend dole school. It is on these dregs that Charles
Russell bases his criticism of the elementary schools.
8
As for his remarks on truancy and school attendance authorities
and magistrates, I am afraid he is about thirty years behind the
times. There are very few truants nowadays and very rarely
does a case have to come before the magistrates.
If he wants to know the real reasons for the existence of the
‘moronic’ class, I can give him the two chief ones. Environment,
and lack of interest on the part of the parents.
Having disposed of his ill-advised criticisms, let us examine
his ‘moronic’ class and its literature. As can be guessed from
this brief survey I have given of the elementary school career of
these boys they have gone from class to class and always been at
the bottom end. At school they know they are regarded as
‘duds.’ At home, they are probably told the same in very pic-
turesque language. So it is not astonishing to find that by the
time they have left school, they have developed an inferiority
complex regarding school work and everything else. Their
reaction in self-defence is to take up the attitude that all school
work is for children, and they are men. Away with books, bring
out the football coupons. There is no doubt, by the way, that
the dole school is unique in the educational system of this country.
Its pupils, although they have just left the elementary school,
indeed, because of that fact, regard themselves as men who have
put away all childish things, and don’t intend to have them
brought out again.
In view of these facts, is it any wonder that Krist, Thurber,
Madame Curie, and Dorothy Sayers, did not go down well? And
as for Lamb’s Tales from Shakespeare, I can’t blame the lads,
but what was a ‘schoolmaster’ doing to let them be burnt?
By this time you will be saying: ‘Oh, so there ’s another side
to the question!’ but I am not finished yet. Although Mr
Russell has not asked for any advice, except from the N.B.C., I
am going to give him some.
Refer to the elementary school once more. Any experienced
teacher can pick out these ‘morons’ by the time they are eleven
years old. In large schools they actually are picked out and
they form what is often known as a ‘special class.’ I have had
charge of such a one for the last ten years. That ’s why Russell’s
criticism hit me where it hurt! Now, Russell, visualize your
‘morons’ four or five years younger and shudder if you like.
Most of them can’t do arithmetic, some can’t write, others can’t
read. What an educational chamber of horrors. They all read,
or have read to them, The Wizard and The Rover.
Since Russell has stuck to literature, so will I. Everybody
knows that the real reason why boys read books for enjoyment
is because they identify themselves with the hero. Unfortunately,
as may be imagined, this type of boy never has been the hero or
the centre of interest. He has always been pushed into the
background as a no-good.
His first reaction, therefore, is to an ordinary book, is to sneer
at the hero and ridicule him. It ’s an example of sour grapes,
and like the fox—he walks away. No books interest him.
My solution was this. Get a book as different as possible
from Eric, or Little by Little. The answer was Huckleberry Finn.
Now Huck is one of Charles Russell’s ‘morons.’ I read it to my
special class, and they lapped it up. Here was a hero just like
themselves, and did they revel in the fact! After that, it was

easy. Once I had gained their confidence by showing that (as
far as they knew) my literary taste coincided with theirs, I had,
and still have, no further trouble. They trust my judgment and
accept my advice regarding reading matter.
Here are a few of the books which I find this type of child
enjoys. But first a word of warning. All books must be ‘edited,’
lengthy descriptions must be cast overboard; give the description
in your own words if you must, but don’t bore them with long-
read descriptions. They must have action! Don’t be afraid of
using different voices, if you can, in dialogue; it helps the thick
’uns to understand more easily. Don’t be afraid of running
over the prelude to a climax twice to make sure they fully appre-
ciate the sequel.
Miss uninteresting chapters out by all means, but don’t forget
to inform the class that you ’ve only read them part of the book.
Practically all my boys have public library tickets which are chiefly
used to obtain the books read in class, so that they can enjoy the
portions missed.
Now, what literature do they appreciate? First, above all,
Huckleberry Finn. Uncle Remus is a favourite, especially if read
in the negro idiom. Strangely enough, The Wind in the Willows
is immensely popular. Toad is a second Donald Duck. Mase-
field’s poetry, and some of his novels are well liked, especially
The Bird of Dawning, which, in my opinion, will one day rival
Treasure Island as a boys’ adventure book. Some of Kipling’s
stories are appreciated. As for RU books, as Mr Russell speci-
fically mentions them, although I did not know RU catered for
‘morons,’ some of them go down well, although he was unfor-
tunate in his choice. The Sword in the Stone, judiciously edited,
made good reading. So did parts of Arundel and Alone. The
others, as I think will be generally agreed, are above the heads of
most young people. Outside the bounds of RU, I could mention
dozens of examples, from Nicholas Nickleby to Emil and the
Detectives.
And in closing I would say that I also teach at an evening
institute and see these boys after leaving school until they reach
the age of eighteen, and I can honestly say that, once started, their
literary taste develops as it should.
Lastly, you may have noticed that I have, throughout this article,
placed ‘moron’ in inverted commas. Why? My answer is
another question. Was Huckleberry Finn a moron?
West Hartlepool. R. W.

Berlin in Blue
A large volume of letters has come to us about Goodbye
to Berlin, and we print a short selection. A fair general
conclusion is that Isherwood’s book both fascinates and
horrifies, and as this description also applies adequately
to the Berlin of the early ’thirties, we must conclude that
Isherwood has done a significant job, and has as well
produced a work of literature.

I should like to say how much I appreciate your March
choice, Goodbye to Berlin. I am a great admirer of Christopher
Isherwood and had read it before but I was particularly glad to
re-read it and to possess a copy. What particularly struck me on
re-reading it was his magnificent power of indirect description.
For example, at the beginning of ‘Sally Bowles,’ Isherwood makes
no specific description of her and yet he brings out—all in con-
versation—a vivid picture of the wayward, nervous, and attrac-
tive slut within two pages of the beginning of the story. His
picture, too, of the neurotic, strange, attractive quality of Germany
just before the Nazi régime is wonderful. I visited this Germany
too in 1929, 1930, and 1931, and fell completely under its spell—
alternately fascinated and horrified. When the Nazis took over,
two months in a little manufacturing town in East Germany, far
away from the tourist routes, showed me Nazism with its surface
off, and I have never gone back.
London, W.C.2 Walter Grieve

While Goodbye to Berlin seemed to me to be richer in the hint of
what it might have been than the embodiment of what it is,
I found it active, alive, and in parts profound. It showed, I
thought, the exactitude of the camera rather than the revealing
brush of the artist; maybe for that reason I found myself viewing
its very live characters as if I were happily seated in those calm
sanctuaries of the wise from which Lucretius loved to watch men
running to and fro, seeking the path of life. It reminded me a
little, though the style and breadth are very, very different, of
Lubbock’s Roman Pictures.
The prose, easy and flowing though it is, startles more than it
satisfies with poetical imagery. A fine concept is too often
damned by a ludicrous or at least incongruous simile. As an
example, ‘birds call with uncanny violence, like alarm-clocks
going off.’ And again, ‘the roe went bucketing over the earth
with wild, rigid jerks, like a grand piano bewitched.’ Forceful
and witty you will perhaps suggest, and I shall be the last to
dissent from that. Yet to me it seemed thereby to have lost in
depth that which it may have gained in surface sparkle.
With all its humour and pathos and force the books is, I think, a
reflection on civilization as we know it, though I am unable to
determine whether the author intended that. Throughout
these sketches the characters are running to and fro seeking the
path of life and, my God! what a path is theirs! The escapades
of the prostitute in the first sketch are admittedly amusing, but
beneath the humour are human lives running to waste amidst a
desert of bricks more sterile than the Sahara itself.
Yet here the book fell short, I thought, and became a little un-
true—a fault inseparable from the photographic presentation
which takes and can take account only of the mere outward
semblance, the misleading façade. Life’s glory and splendour
comes from within, and is as likely to be found on a dung-heap
as in the damask-hung halls of the cloud-capped towers and
gorgeous palaces. The book would have been redeemed by one
character with some pretentions to serenity.
I would not call this a great book, but I think that you did very
well to choose it. May you keep the banners of enlightenment
fluttering bravely through the dark hours!
West Wickham. G. E. Whitfield

Tom Hanlin seems to have a pessimistic view of our ultimate
end, but he ’s wrong. They are working men (I presume that by

THE FICTION OF HISTORY
One: The Nineteenth Century
by H. G. Andrews
The first of a group of articles on the historical principle
in fiction. This survey concerns the 19th-century historical
fictionists.

HERODOTUS, the Greek of Halicarnassus, born
under Persian domination, was the Father of all His-
torians. He begot bastards as well as lawful progeny
in his parenthood. But who shall lay it upon a man’s
shoulders, as a whip, that he lacks a surname? Truly
there are as many knaves born in wedlock as out of it.
In a like figure, there have been as many historians who
craftily sidestepped from the strait way of Truth as
novelists who gaily romanticized those whose acts
made History. The Historian interprets only the facts
of the case; the Novelist must make the dead live
again.
There are two great classes of historical novelists.
The one takes up its particular variety of the lay-
figures of fiction (hero, heroine, villain, attendants, all
sumptuously arrayed in fancy dress, complete with the
usual properties: cane, rapier, oaths), and sets them
down against a flat sketchy backcloth of historical fact.
Those of the other class create their characters within
the truth of humanity, and give them their being in a
roundy world of three—nay, four—dimensions; which,
in the sweat of research, they have re-created out of
the chronicles of time past. The former writers do
not so much seek to popularize history as make an
escape into the dimness of a bygone age, where they
may give full rein to an extreme romanticism. They
have not learned what from the outset the others
realized: that the true historical novel must combine
the universal of life with the particular of history in
such a fine balance of proportions as will present an
always acceptable vraisemblance in the name of funda-
mental humanity.
These classes, then, are divided upon the edge of
their intention; their difference being in the initial
impulse; with only the latter having the ‘high serious-
ness’ which Matthew Arnold postulated as the true
spirit of great literature.
Each of these divisions are subdivided, according to
matter. One writer invents a whole dramatis personae.
Another sets his own principals among the great
realities. A third moves across his pages the re-
animated shadows of those realities; principals as well
as subsidiaries named from fact.
Actually these subdivisions are immaterial to the
final result, as art. It is only when the dramatic
movement of the plot is inextricably woven into its
particular historical setting, when the characters are
dependent upon and inevitably moulded by their
peculiar historical environment, that we have an
example of the greater art of historical fiction. Then
is the bastard greater than the lawfully born; the nove-
list greater than the historian.
But we must exercise considerable care on the ques-

[Abbildung: Robert Louis Stevenson, after a bronze relief by Augustus St Gaudens, 1887]

tion of Romance. It is in its extreme form that it is
fatal to the classical ‘high seriousness.’ It is a kind
of tenth Muse, possessed of a magical virtue: an
essential element which, if properly employed, may
enliven and articulate the features and the members
of the dead. In the words of Shorthouse (his preface
to John Inglesant) ‘it is only when these dry details (of
historical research) are touched by the enchanter’s
wand (Romance) that they strike with any sense of
reality—that these dry bones assume any appearance
of life, any attribute of love, or pity, or even of hate.’
If, by and large, it is our considered judgment that
Scott was the greatest English-writing exponent of
the art of historical fiction, at the same time, we admit
that it was not he who produced (to date) the greatest
example of it. The honour of that must go to Charles
Reade, who, in The Cloister and the Hearth (1861)
produced a much finer work than the B.B.C.’s melo-
dramatic interpretation gave us to think. The end of
the Middle Ages was a raw, colourful, brutal period;
the rude savage had scarcely begun to learn of the
civilizing art of letters. The great Greek and Latin
literatures lay dead and buried; their resurrection was
not yet. Reade knew the age to the last chapter of its
history; for years he had lined his forehead with the
study of it in every aspect (architecture, clothes,
arms, and armour; transport, trade, crafts, medicine,
food—what was there he had not fully annotated and
filed away in his pigeon-holed workshop?) before he
dared set pen to paper. In consequence the colourful
brutal story is woven into and made one with the crude
tapestry of an exact detail; and every part of its drawing
is clean, proportioned, and authentic.
Between Scott and Reade came Bulwer Lytton,
whose interest in the historical novel was exhausted
by 1850; Thackeray with Henry Esmond, a work standing
high in the company of the best; Charles Kingsley
11

HISTORICAL PRINCIPLE: XIXth CENTURY (continued)
with Westward Ho! and Hereward the Wake (both filled
with romantic virility yet misleading in spirit); Harrison
Ainsworth, scattering about his pages brilliant des-
criptions and incredible conversations; and minor
others, generally forgotten.
Two years after The Cloister and the Hearth came
Whyte Melville’s The Gladiators, which portrayed the
steadfast deaths of Christian slaves in the arena. A
little later followed Blackmore’s Lorna Doone, but the
historical interest of this book is of small weight.
More than a decade passed before Walter Pater wrote
Marius the Epicurean, a work of exceptional merit, by
reason of its style and its account of the spiritual
problems of the age of Marcus Aurelius; and during
this time the name of Shorthouse was heard on the
tongues and in the ears of the discerning, although
John Inglesant was never a popular success, its main
interest being mystical rather than historical.
In 1888, William Morris published A Dream of
John Ball, a fine romance faithful to the spirit and the
flesh of the fourteenth century, and beautifully told.
Morris’s art had that element of true magic about it,
whereby he made the past to live again, and we in the
midst of it, alive with an urgent awareness. The self-
same enchantment quickens his verse as well as his
prose: witness these lines from the Prologue to The
Earthly Paradise.
‘. . . behind us lay
The walls of Vannes, white in the summer day,
The reapers whistled, the brown maidens sung,
As on the wain the topmost sheaf they hung,
The swallow wheeled above high up in air,
And midst the labour all was sweet and fair;
When on the winding road between the fields
I saw a glittering line of spears and shields,
And pleased therewith called out to someone by
E’en as I could; he scarce for fear could cry
“The French, the French!” and turned and ran . . .’

Stevenson’s romantic genius was intensely attracted
to the eighteenth century. Beyond that time he was
lost. The Black Arrow, historically a failure, shows
how little sympathy he had for the Middle Ages. At
the same time, Kidnapped, its sequel, Catriona, The
Master of Ballantrae, and the fragment of his master-
piece, Weir of Hermiston, reveal how deep were the
insight and affection he had for Scott’s greatest period.

J. H. SHORTHOUSE. John Inglesant: a Romance. Macmillan, 2/-.
WALTER SCOTT [1771-1832]. Old Mortality. Probably the
finest of Scott’s many historical novels. Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
CHARLES READE [1814-84]. The Cloister and the Hearth. A
romance of the Middle Ages; the hero is the father of Erasmus,
one of the leaders of the Renaissance. Intro. by A. C. Swin-
burne. Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
BULWER LYTTON [1803-73]. Harold, the last of the Saxon
Kings (1848). Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
WM. THACKERAY [1811-63]. The History of Henry Esmond,
Esq. (1852). Leaving romanticized historical fiction behind him
Thackeray started a new standard of historical realism. [Sequel:
The Virginians.] Murray, 1/6
CHARLES KINGSLEY [1819-75]. Westward Ho! An impas-
sioned national saga portraying idealized heroes.
Hereward the Wake. The hero is a wild unruly youth, half a
Dane. Both books are in Everyman’s Library, 2/6 each.
HARRISON AINSWORTH [1805-82]. Old St Paul’s. Rich
local and historical colour, portraits of celebrities in 17th-century
London. Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
GEORGE WHYTE MELVILLE [1821-78]. The Gladiators.
Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
R. D. BLACKMORE [1825-1900]. Lorna Doone. A return to
romantic historical fiction. Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
WALTER PATER [1839-94]. Marius the Epicurean (1885).
Everyman’s Library, 2/6.
WM. MORRIS [1834-96]. The Dream of John Ball (1888). An
idyll of the Kentish rising (1381) showing author’s social ideas.
Longmans, 1/6.
R. L. STEVENSON [1850-94]. The Master of Ballantrae (1888)
and The Black Arrow (1880). In one vol. Everyman’s Library,
2/6. Catriona. Cassell, 2/6. Kidnapped. Cassell, 2/6. Weir
of Hermiston. Chatto, 1/6.

EDITORIAL: CHANDOS PLACE, CHARING
CROSS, LONDON, WC2
LOOKING THE BOOK
WORLD IN THE EYE

WE HAVE OF LATE relieved our members of
words about our selection policy. The page of scrap-
book turns (it has turned month by month for nearly
three years) and words have been replaced by deeds;
these two shelves must tell the tale.
But the claims of a rival club compel us to make
one point. It is this fact: that our choices have in all
been made from the lists of about twenty-five publishing
houses! Between last May and this month our selections and
additionals bear the imprints of fifteen publishing
houses! A simple process of deduction must bring
any observer first to the important conclusion that no
single publisher nor any group of publishers controls,
modulates, or orientates RU selection policy.
The second obvious deduction is that RU seeks
widely and effortlessly for its choices. We actually do
confront each year an output of something like ten
thousand new books; and we attempt a selection. We
start each month with our minds, our ideas, our actions
unfettered by any editorial consideration but the
satisfaction of our members. If our policy leads us
to make mistakes—and we admit that at times our
opinions of virtue in literature do not coincide with
informed opinion among our members—then, alone
among book clubs, we let criticism ring uncensored in
the columns of ‘The Open Forum.’
If the setting of standards of literary or information
value, a concern that integrity of writing should count
before reputation, innate virtue before notoriety, and a
recollection of the way in which the best-sellers of
yesterday become the intolerables of to-day (remember
The Young Visitors and If Winter Comes?), if this does
not lead to the choosing of currently fashionable fiction,
what would you?
This talk of standards sounds highfalutin and
pompous; but consider the alternative. If we let real
choosing go hang and simply bargained for ‘what the
public wants’ we should inevitably find ourselves
giving the public what it wanted at an estimate of its
lowest common denominator. Our action would be
resolved into one of handing out to a set of unsophisti-
cated children the showiest of literary sweetmeats.
It depends on your respect for your members.
As to our sincerity in this matter, which you have a
right to challenge, since RU is a limited company and is
not, as one publishing concern describes itself, ‘a
privately-operated public-utility company,’ we ask that
it shall be remembered of us that we were first in the
field, had the opportunity of doing the kind of book-
selling that brings astronomical membership and sleek
motor-cars. That opportunity knocked at our door
and found us away.
Our reward is in the loyalty of our members; in

[Abbildung: Bücherregal]

RU since 1937: ‘A little library growing larger every
year is an honourable part of a man’s history’
a relation charged with a unique quality of affection;
and in the receipt of letters from our members which,
because of their moving terms, we dare not print.
When we are scolded we are scolded as an errant child
and when circumstances force a resignation that
resignation is usually tendered with infinite reluctance.
Each member considers himself part-owner of RU;
the first person plural is the coin of our realm.
As a result our membership remains unaffected by
the growth of new book clubs. At between 23,000 and
25,000—and if we counted additional book purchases
and Basis Book purchases we could put it higher—it
has not slumped when the membership of every other
serious book club has taken a header. It is maintained
even in face of the constant drain upon our membership
of the Military Service Act, which will later touch a
vast proportion of our members; and will almost
decimate RU staff.
If we are sad at times, it is rather for democracy
than for ourselves that we mourn—a ‘ceiling’ of
25,000 members is nothing to get excited about: but
even this relative failure may be altered by what P. G.
Wodehouse calls Time the Great Healer, by events, or
by the continued and communicated evangelism of
our members.
J. B.

I SEEK IN SHADOW
by Stefan Zweig
A fundamental interest in motives, a seeking for the
sources of action, a long exploratory analysis, and a
compression of results: that is Stefan Zweig’s method
whether he is writing a biography of Romain Rolland,
of Marie Antoinette, of Magellan—or whether he is planning
his first long novel Beware of Pity. Behind the real or
imagined subject stands the artist, noting, observing, pon-
dering. The result is a work of art, a sense of the pulse
of humanity; and time is powerless to date it.

IF I HAD TO CONFESS which quality has been
predominant to make me a writer, I believe that I
would have to name one which generally is not very
much appreciated in private life—a great and insatiable
curiosity. I cannot remember a time of my life when
this curiosity to know and to understand peoples,
countries, men, and history has not been burning
within me, and I feel it to-day as active as in my child-
hood. Nothing exasperates me more than not to
understand a man or the motives of his action, and to
explain these hidden motives to myself I know no
better way than to study them thoroughly by writing
a book. A psychological problem is as attractive for
me in a living man as in an historical person; so my
novels and biographies come out of the same source
and are complementary to each other.
Being inquisitive in many ways I have written a great
deal, and what I have published is in reality only a part
of it. For in my endeavour to explain a character or
a problem to myself I write at first on my own pleasure.
Then I begin to shorten, to leave out everything which
is not strictly necessary, and books which have in my
own manuscript more than a hundred thousand words
appear when printed with only thirty or forty thousand.
This constant and repeated work of concentration is
what I like most and I believe that a part of the
success of my books is due to the fact that I have
tried to eliminate much that is superfluous. Many
of my stories (as Amok, Letter of an Unknown Woman)
are in fact concentrated full-size novels, and each of
them has been dramatized and filmed just as if they had
been volumes of a hundred thousand words.
The only full-size novel which I have written till
to-day is Beware of Pity. Here it did not seem permitted
to me to concentrate only on the pure story; I had to
develop the leit-motiv: how pity, one of the finest and
noblest feelings of the human soul, can become, when
exaggerated, extremely dangerous. Furthermore, I felt
it my duty to revive for an international public our pre-
war Austria, which I have known and which is doomed
never to rise again in its specific form of culture, with
all its charm, sentimentality, and refinement.

[Abbildung: S O S I]

Several RU choices are now out-of-print rarities; among them
The Ascent of Nanda Devi, Madame Curie, and The Importance of Living.
For each of these we have had urgent requests. On behalf of
members who particularly need copies of either these RU will
gladly pass on to enquirers the name of anyone who would be
willing to effect a private sale.
14
About my private life I do not like to speak. I have
lived in many countries and it is my conviction that in
order to see much one should not be seen oneself.
So I endeavour to make my presence everywhere as
anonymous as possible. I seldom go into society, I
make no public appearance, I give no lectures—except
last year the big lecture tour throughout the United
States which every author has to perform once—and
I hope to be able to continue to observe and to study
men and peoples without being observed myself.
My home is now in Bath, one of the quietest and most
artistic towns of England, favoured and loved by all
the great writers of the past, and I delight to live in her
shadow.

[Handschriftliche Unterschrift: Stefan Zweig]

[Abbildung: Porträt von Charles Darwin]

Charles Darwin from a portrait by John Collier

Origin of Species. Time has dimmed the splendour of that
achievement by rendering his hypothesis a commonplace
of universal thought but at that date, in that confused and
inquiring world of groping scientists, the discerning noted
that the scattered strands of research into the history of man

NEXT MONTH’S CHOICE (RU’S FIRST
FULL SCIENTIFIC) IS A BOOK ABOUT
THE PROPER STUDY BY
Stanley Casson:
THE DISCOVERY OF MAN
(Hamish Hamilton 12s. 6d., RU 2s. 6d.)

My intention in writing The Discovery of Man was to
sketch the outlines of a single story—the strange and
fascinating story of how Man has come to be studied
objectively.
It will remain always one of the strangest of all
enigmas of human history that however proper may be
the study of Mankind by Man, yet it is invariably the
ultimate study in which he indulges. For millennia
man has, in fact, taken peculiar pains to study almost
anything but himself. He has avoided self-analysis
and the study of human origins and developments as if
they were the plague. In this he was moved, I think,
by that curious but fundamental motive which is in-
herent in human nature, which inhibits a human being
from self-examination lest he be charged by his fellows
with a kind of morbid indecency.
Man, to live and survive, must in the earliest stages
of his existence be completely objective. He has to
meet dangers from without and dissensions from
within: he is always at the frontier posts facing exter-
mination of individual or species. Who, as defender
of his species, can afford to turn his eye inwards in self-
contemplation, when his visible and tangible aid is
needed by the community to which he belongs to
combat the dangers that attend?
Thus the practical inventor, the working scientist,
and the organizer pure and simple, all alike working
for human advancement, have priority over any mere
researcher into human faculties or origins. Curiosity
into human matters per se belongs to a stage when
society is more secure, more confident, and more
capable of recognizing self-consciousness as a virtue
rather than as a dangerous luxury. For all inquiry into
human relations and origins is a form of self-con-
sciousness.
We still use the term ‘self-conscious’ as a term of
reproach, ‘self-analysis’ as an occupation for the
unstable and unworthy, and in all our curricula of
schools and the hardest objectivity is encouraged in the
young and considered an essential ingredient of the
mature. ‘Subjective,’ as a term, has none of the merits
accorded to its opposite, ‘objective’: ‘impersonal’ has
some quality superior to ‘personal.’
Here, indeed, is a long-standing prejudice in the
human race, born of the need for survival, bred in the
hard school of selection. It accounts for a hundred
strange aberrations of the mob, for a thousand minor
aspects of persecution and repression. In the early
stages of human history when the need for such self-
consciousness or analysis becomes so evident that not
all the reactions of established authority can subdue it,
such activities are relegated to a particular value and
sphere in social organization. They are dealt with by
medicine-men, by an organized priest-craft, and by
publicly appointed experts.
As human progress recedes so does human curiosity,
for as danger increases so luxuries must go by the
board. During all those intervals in progress, when
civilization receded or collapsed, the status of the sub-
jective man was degraded. In the Dark Ages of history
artists, students of human custom and origins, and those
who speculated into the purpose of life itself vanished
like mist in the general storm that beset humanity.
The fall of the Greco-Roman world brought crashing
with it all the art and research that Greeks and Romans
alike had laboriously built up. For a thousand years
man remained obdurately and deliberately incurious
of himself, and attempts to renew the achievements of
the ancients were ruthlessly destroyed at birth.
To-day, when that grave possibility of the destruction
or degradation of the race is again to the fore, we see a
recrudescence of those same inhibitions, of those same
repressions. Scientists are pressed to confine their
labours to problems that promote human protection or
human defence: even psychologists, those of scientists
who are concerned with the most intimate aspects of
man, are encouraged to devote their abilities and their
actions to the detection of crime, the control of propa-
ganda and the repair of war-scarred humanity. Inter-
national security alone can give scientific research its
proper backing and the researchers a status which
leaves them outside the tides of current affairs.
Undoubtedly to-day we live in an age of recession.
And such ages have occurred throughout the course
of history—have occurred and been overcome. For
human curiosity in human origins and development is
a factor in society as well as in the individual which far
outweighs human incuriosity. It invariably buds and
grows again and is the hardiest plant of all. The
curiosity of one man can infect a nation, and the deter-
mination of individuals to explore their own past over-
rides in all ages the power of society to suppress them.
The history of the early Renaissance is illuminated by
single stars which radiate their energy even in the
interstellar space of religious persecution. The last
five hundred years of our history shows us how the
single stars became groups and, in the end, constella-
tions. If from time to time they were obscured by the
passing mists of obscurantism and reaction, it was the
mists that passed, not the stars that were extinguished.
S. C.

A BARGAIN FOR
QUICK SCOTS
An All MacDiarmid-Grassic Gibbon
Production

In the February Readers’ News a Scottish RU member
who was ashamed to reveal his identity pleaded for ‘a
slight Scottish essence in RU choices.’ As it happened
we had lately heard of an amazing compilation by two
individuals bearing the outlandish names of Lewis
Grassic Gibbon and Hugh MacDiarmid. It was called
Scottish Scene or The Intelligent Man’s Guide to Albyn.
We hastened to examine this book, stated as being
composed by one author ‘in a pleasant village near
London, the other in the sound of the running seas
by the Shetlands’—a sentence which hardly conforms
to Compton Mackenzie’s assertion that ‘to an English-
man something is what it is called: to a Scotsman some-
thing is what it is.’ The result of our examination was
to convince us that Scottish Scene is like nothing else
in literature.
It would appear to the casual observer to be an
anthology of nearly three hundred pages consisting of
essays, pieces of drama, poems, and sketches by divers
hands, all playing in a mordant key of ‘Scots wha hae,’
interspersed by a ‘news-reel’ culled from the magazines
and newspapers of Scotland.
Closer examination reveals that Messrs Gibbon and
MacDiarmid have written the entire collection them-
selves. They appear to have set about a kind of

[Abbildung: Schere]

clip and post at once
To Readers’ Union Limited
Dunham’s Lane Letchworth
Hertfordshire
Send me
.........copies of SCOTTISH SCENE
by Lewis Grassic Gibbon and Hugh
MacDiarmid at 2s 6d

My supplier is:

RU Number (if any).........................

literary game of draughts with Scotland as the battle-
ground, or perhaps the analogy of a relay race would
pay a better tribute to their stamina. Mr M.’s first
move is a poem entitled Scotland; and Mr G. follows with
an essay on The Antique Scene, and Mr M. counters with
The Modern Scene. There follows from Mr G. a short
story called Greenden, reeking with the Scottish dialect.
Then there are studies of representative Scots, these
being Major (‘Social Credit’) Douglas and Ramsay
MacDonald. Added are sketches, alternately by
Mr M. and Mr G., of Scottish towns: Aberdeen, Dun-
dee, Glasgow, Edinburgh. And so on, from poem to
document in great variety.
The game is played throughout in great spirits, and
concludes with an envoi:
Peter Pan nae langer oor deity ’ll be
And oor boast an endless infantilism.
Away wi’ the auld superstitions. Let the
Sun up at last
And hurl a’ sic spooks into their proper abysm.
Which sentiment, with our imperfect knowledge of the
language, we heartily applaud.
It is fair to add that only a small portion of the volume
is written in this execrable way, and our impression is
that any Englishman will find in Scottish Scene the
perfect antidote to the land o’ cakes school of Scottish
thought and will possess on purchase a volume which
contains from page 1 to page 288 ‘some slight Scottish
essence.’
Our interrogation of the publisher of Scottish Scene,
Messrs Hutchinson, produced a sheaf of favourable
reviews, which spoke in surprisingly praiseful terms of
the volume, and referred to the authors MacDiarmid
and Gibbon as if somebody should have heard of them.
He also informed us that his stock was a mere five
hundred sheets. Without much show of bargaining,
and thus enlightened, we purchased the lot, have bound
them very nicely in cloth boards, and offer them to first-
comers, irrespective of nationality, at half a crown.
Please, if you require a copy of Scottish Scene, post
to us the coupon alongside—there will be no card
canvass.
And after this nobody will be able to say that we don’t
do our best for all Scottish gentlemen.
J. B.

WHAT THE CRITICS SAY
Of Lewis Grassic Gibbon, Ivor Brown says: ‘A mind
which passionately apprehends, interprets, and assesses
the Scotland of to-day. He has put himself at the head
of the Scottish revival in letters.’ Compton Mackenzie’s
verdict on Hugh MacDiarmid is: ‘The testament of the
most powerful intellectually and emotionally fertilizing force Scotland
has known since the death of Burns.’
